reflection.theme: The Saturday You Couldn't Have Before
reflection.quote: The least of things with a meaning is worth more in life than the greatest of things without it.
reflection.reading:
Saturday in June used to be a write-off. Either I was already drinking by noon or I was paying for Friday and calling it rest. The whole day had a shape — recover, refuel, ruin it again. I called it freedom, but freedom doesn't usually leave you on the couch by 4 p.m. negotiating with yourself about whether to shower.

What I didn't understand at 22 was that the life I wanted wasn't going to arrive in some big cinematic moment. It was going to arrive in Saturdays. Quiet ones, full ones, the kind where you wake up early because you want to, not because anxiety dragged you out of bed at 6 a.m. The kind where you go for a hike, or get coffee with a friend who actually knows you, or do absolutely nothing — and the nothing feels earned instead of haunted. That's the life. Not the highlight reel. The Tuesdays and Saturdays.

Jung wasn't writing about sobriety, but he was writing about this: a small thing you actually mean is worth more than a big thing you're performing. Today is small. Today is also yours. You're not killing time until the real life starts. You're in it. The job is to notice — to be where your feet are, on a Saturday in June, awake.
reflection.meditation: Where are my feet today, and what's actually in front of me?

affirmations: I am awake for my own life today, and that is not a small thing.
Today I get to choose what I do with the hours in front of me.
I am building something with these ordinary days, even when I can't see it yet.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Saturday I'm actually present for. Grateful for the version of me who decided, however many years ago, that he was done — and for everyone who showed up while I figured out how to stay. The coffee tastes better. The morning is quieter. The day is mine to spend.
gratitude.closing: One more day, awake and accounted for.
